experiment: Task 18: simulator cost-model fixes for grad_ar/dxe convergence
phase: Phase 2 simulator scoring with improved cost model
simulator_changes: MockTorch.cat / MockTorch.stack now record output bytes (was 0).

CollectiveSimulator.estimate_latency and benchmark_xla_candidate_generic walk events in chronological order; back-to-back collectives amortize dispatch overhead (subsequent issues in a run pay ~10% of full setup).
cat / stack now get bytes-aware cost (floor + bytes / sequential memcpy bw).

TrackedTensor arithmetic dunders (__mul__/__add__/__sub__/__truediv__/__neg__/__mod__) now record into the op counter (was silent), so per-tensor-then-consume patterns differ from back-to-back-then-consume in the event stream.
Added _FUSED_ELEMENTWISE_OPS category whose ops get the per-op floor cost (reflects XLA HLO elementwise fusion) but still break the back-to-back collective run.
effect_on_ranking: Before the cost-model fixes per_tensor_loop and async_back_to_back both scored 11360 us — the simulator could not distinguish them. After: per_tensor_loop 11392 us, async_back_to_back 5812 us — a 2x sim-time differentiation that captures EFA pipelining. flat_single_ar still wins by ~10% over async because the test-case tensors are small (4 KB cat cost is negligible at this scale).
why_not_answer_leaking: Each cost-model change is physics-grounded: torch.cat literally copies bytes through HBM; EFA literally pipelines back-to-back NIC issues; XLA literally fuses adjacent elementwise ops into one kernel. The simulator never inspects which algorithm it is scoring — it only walks the recorded event stream. The agent still has to discover which algorithm best exploits these facts, and the simulator still picks a different winner from the HW winner (sim winner flat_single_ar at 5286 us; HW winner bucket_32mb at 1.180 ms) — proof that we did not bias the score toward async_back_to_back.
residual_gap: HW shows bucket (1.180 ms), async (1.203 ms), and flat (1.237 ms) all within 5% of each other, while the simulator has them at 5286 / 5812 / 5286 us — a 10% gap between async and the 1-AR variants. Bounded by: (a) the dispatch-overhead constant (set by the LLM Phase-1 profiler) potentially under-pricing single ARs at large bytes; (b) the simulator not yet modeling that XLA fuses N elementwise muls into a single kernel (mul cost is the per-op floor, but per-op recording still overestimates fused-elementwise total). These are out of scope for this iteration.
